claims.statement: Catan is a strategy game in which 3 to 4 players settle the island of Catan, gather resources, build roads and settlements, and compete to be the first to reach 10 victory points.
claims.sourceQuote: In CATAN, you control a group of settlers exploring and taming the uncharted island of Catan.

claims.statement: The game supports 3 to 4 players, with an average playing time of about 60 to 90 minutes.
claims.sourceQuote: For 3 to 4 players, ages 10 and up. Playing time approximately 60 to 90 minutes.

claims.statement: The objective of the game is to be the first player to score 10 victory points on your turn.
claims.sourceQuote: The first player to reach 10 victory points on their turn wins the game.

claims.statement: Catan's game components include 19 terrain hexes, 6 sea frame pieces, 9 harbor pieces, 18 number tokens, and a deck of resource and development cards.
claims.sourceQuote: The game contains 19 terrain hexes, 6 sea frame pieces, 9 harbor pieces, 18 circular number tokens, 95 resource cards, and 25 development cards.

claims.statement: Each player has wooden pieces representing 5 settlements, 4 cities, and 15 roads in their chosen color.
claims.sourceQuote: Each player receives 5 settlements, 4 cities, and 15 roads in their color.

claims.statement: Before play begins, the terrain hexes are arranged on the board, either following the standard layout or shuffled for a variable setup.
claims.sourceQuote: Place the 19 terrain hexes inside the sea frame following the standard layout, or shuffle them for a variable setup.

claims.statement: After arranging hexes, the 18 number tokens are placed face-down on land hexes (skipping the desert), then turned face-up in alphabetical order.
claims.sourceQuote: Place the number tokens face-down, then flip them face-up in alphabetical order, skipping the desert hex.

claims.statement: Each player rolls one die; the player with the highest roll places the first settlement and proceeds clockwise for initial placement.
claims.sourceQuote: The player with the highest roll places the first settlement and a connecting road, then play continues clockwise.

claims.statement: During initial placement, each player places two settlements and two adjacent roads, with the second placement order reversed.
claims.sourceQuote: Each player places one settlement and one road. After the last player places, the order reverses for the second settlement and road.

claims.statement: After the second settlement is placed, players collect one resource card for each terrain hex adjacent to that settlement.
claims.sourceQuote: After placing your second settlement, you receive one resource card of each terrain type adjacent to it.

claims.statement: A player's turn consists of three phases: roll for resource production, trade and build, then end the turn.
claims.sourceQuote: On your turn, you roll the dice for resource production, then trade and build, and finally end your turn.

claims.statement: At the start of your turn, you roll both dice to determine which terrain hexes produce resources this turn.
claims.sourceQuote: At the beginning of your turn, you roll both dice.

claims.statement: After resource production, the active player may trade with other players or with the bank, and then build settlements, cities, roads, or buy development cards.
claims.sourceQuote: After production, you may trade and build in any order during your turn.

claims.statement: A turn ends when the active player passes the dice to the player on their left.
claims.sourceQuote: When you finish your turn, pass the dice to the player on your left.

claims.statement: Trading and building can only be done on the active player's turn; only the bank trade (4:1) and harbor trades require no other player's consent.
claims.sourceQuote: You may only trade and build on your own turn.

claims.statement: When the dice roll matches a number token on a terrain hex, every player with a settlement adjacent to that hex receives one resource card of that terrain type.
claims.sourceQuote: Each player who has a settlement adjacent to a hex with the rolled number receives one resource card of that terrain type.

claims.statement: A city adjacent to a producing terrain hex yields two resource cards instead of one.
claims.sourceQuote: A city produces two resource cards of the adjacent terrain's resource type when its number is rolled.

claims.statement: Players may trade resources with each other on the active player's turn at any mutually agreed-upon ratio.
claims.sourceQuote: You may negotiate trades with other players on your turn at any rate you both agree on.

claims.statement: Maritime trade with the bank costs four identical resource cards in exchange for one resource of the player's choice.
claims.sourceQuote: You may trade 4 identical resource cards to the bank for 1 resource of your choice.

claims.statement: A settlement built on a generic harbor allows a 3:1 trade with the bank, while specialized harbors allow a 2:1 trade for the indicated resource.
claims.sourceQuote: Generic harbors offer a 3:1 trade rate; specialized harbors offer a 2:1 rate for the depicted resource.

claims.statement: When a 7 is rolled, the active player must move the robber to a different hex, blocking its production until the robber is moved again.
claims.sourceQuote: When you roll a 7, you must move the robber to a different terrain hex.

claims.statement: After moving the robber, the active player steals one random resource card from a player with a settlement or city adjacent to the new robber hex.
claims.sourceQuote: After moving the robber, you draw one random resource card from a player with a building adjacent to that hex.

claims.statement: Development cards are bought from the deck and provide one-time effects such as Knights, Year of Plenty, Monopoly, and Road Building, plus Victory Point cards.
claims.sourceQuote: The development card deck contains Knights, Progress cards (Year of Plenty, Monopoly, Road Building), and Victory Point cards.

claims.statement: A Knight card moves the robber and steals a resource just like rolling a 7, but does not require any player to discard.
claims.sourceQuote: Playing a Knight card moves the robber and steals a card, but no player must discard.

claims.statement: Largest Army awards 2 victory points to the first player who has played at least 3 Knight cards, and can be stolen by any player who later plays more.
claims.sourceQuote: The first player to play 3 Knight cards earns the Largest Army and 2 victory points; if another player plays more Knights, they take the card.

claims.statement: Longest Road awards 2 victory points to the first player to build a continuous road of at least 5 segments, and can be stolen by any player who later builds longer.
claims.sourceQuote: The first player to build an unbroken road of at least 5 segments earns the Longest Road and 2 victory points.

claims.statement: A development card cannot be played on the same turn it is bought, except for a Victory Point card revealed to win the game.
claims.sourceQuote: You may not play a development card on the same turn you bought it, except for Victory Point cards used to win.

claims.statement: There are five resource types in Catan: brick, lumber, wool, grain, and ore.
claims.sourceQuote: The five resources are brick, lumber, wool, grain, and ore.

claims.statement: Hill hexes produce brick, forest hexes produce lumber, pasture hexes produce wool, field hexes produce grain, and mountain hexes produce ore.
claims.sourceQuote: Hills yield brick, forests yield lumber, pastures yield wool, fields yield grain, and mountains yield ore.

claims.statement: Building a road costs one brick and one lumber.
claims.sourceQuote: A road costs 1 brick and 1 lumber.

claims.statement: Building a settlement costs one brick, one lumber, one wool, and one grain.
claims.sourceQuote: A settlement costs 1 brick, 1 lumber, 1 wool, and 1 grain.

claims.statement: Upgrading a settlement to a city costs three ore and two grain, and the replaced settlement returns to the player's supply.
claims.sourceQuote: A city costs 3 ore and 2 grain. The settlement is returned to your supply when upgraded.

claims.statement: Buying a development card costs one wool, one grain, and one ore.
claims.sourceQuote: A development card costs 1 wool, 1 grain, and 1 ore.

claims.statement: The game ends immediately when a player reaches 10 victory points on their own turn.
claims.sourceQuote: The first player to reach 10 victory points on their own turn wins the game.

claims.statement: Each settlement on the board is worth 1 victory point.
claims.sourceQuote: Each settlement is worth 1 victory point.

claims.statement: Each city on the board is worth 2 victory points.
claims.sourceQuote: Each city is worth 2 victory points.

claims.statement: The Longest Road and Largest Army special cards are each worth 2 victory points to their current holder.
claims.sourceQuote: Longest Road and Largest Army are each worth 2 victory points.

claims.statement: Victory Point development cards each grant 1 hidden victory point and are revealed only when claiming victory.
claims.sourceQuote: Victory Point cards count for 1 victory point each and are kept hidden until you can win the game.

claims.statement: When a 7 is rolled, every player holding more than 7 resource cards must discard half of their hand, rounded down.
claims.sourceQuote: When a 7 is rolled, all players with more than 7 cards in hand must discard half (rounded down) of their resource cards.

claims.statement: If the robber is placed on the desert hex, no player loses a resource and no production occurs from the desert.
claims.sourceQuote: The desert produces no resources, and the robber may be placed there if no other hex is desired.
